sections.paragraph:
Dan 3:7 and פְּסַנְתֵּרִין Dan 3:5, 10 , 15 the Greek word ψαλτήριον (in the LXX. often for נֶבֶל, כִּנֹּור) being adopted in Chald., with the interchange of l and n; it is of the singular number, since-(a) it is joined with other names of instruments which are put in the singular; and-(b) it does not answer to the Greek ψαλτήρ, which signifies harper, but to ψαλτήριον. The Greek ιον, in Oriental languages, either becomes în (compare κοινόβιον, Syr. Kanobín), or altogether is rejected; of which we have an instance in this very word, which is now called in Egypt سنطير (for psantîr); see Villoteau in Descr. de l’Egypte, vi. p. 426. See also the observations of Hengstenberg on this word, in his Authenticity of Daniel, p. 15.

I.
